experiences.intentions:
Player 0: As a Servant, I have no special knowledge yet. On round 1 quest 1, a 2-person team including myself is generally reasonable, and rejecting early without evidence gives little information. Approving preserves tempo and does not reveal much beyond a standard good-faith opening vote.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 0 and 3 are not confirmed but this proposed team [0,3] contains no evil players from my knowledge. As evil in round 1 team voting, the optimal play is usually to approve plausible all-good teams sometimes to avoid exposing coordination and to let good potentially gain only limited info, especially since a reject on a clean team could look suspicious. My vote itself reveals little if I approve here, while rejecting a non-evil team immediately may help good identify resistance patterns.
Player 2: As Assassin, I know Player 1 is my evil teammate, so team [0,3] contains no known evil and is likely all good. In round 1, an all-good 2-person team is bad for evil because it can secure an early success and give good players trust signals. Voting reject is optimal to try to prevent a clean good quest and to avoid helping good coordination. My vote itself gives limited information; an early reject is normal enough and helps evil more than approving does.
Player 3: No role-specific info yet beyond being Good Servant; with no quest history, [0,3] includes me and is a standard low-risk opening team in 5-player Avalon. As Good, approving an opening team with myself on it is optimal to generate information, and my vote reveals little beyond willingness to test a team I selected.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know players 1 and 2 are evil, so players 0 and 3 are the only players I currently believe good besides me. On quest 1, a 2-person team of [0,3] excludes known evil and is the best available team. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a clean team, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.

Player 0: Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 look somewhat good though one could still be evil hiding. Adding 4 to that successful core is a reasonable exploratory team for Quest 2. As a good Servant, I want to approve teams that contain previously successful members and avoid unnecessary rejections early. My vote reveals mild trust in 3 and openness to testing 4, which helps good gather info without much downside.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, and I am evil. The previous successful quest was [0,3], so at least one of 0 or 3 may be good, but success does not clear anyone because evil can choose pass. This proposed team [0,3,4] excludes both known evils, so it is very likely all-good and would increase the good side to 2 successes if approved. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to reject strong-looking all-good teams, especially one excluding both evil players. My reject signals some suspicion but that is acceptable; approving would help good too much.
Player 2: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate, so the proposed team [0,3,4] contains no evil players and would likely succeed, moving Good to 2 successes. Last quest [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 may be read as good by others; adding 4 could also generate more good trust if this passes. As evil in a team voting phase, I should try to prevent clean all-good teams from going through, especially on quest 2. Rejecting gives no hard information because evil often hide votes, but my vote against an all-good team helps my side more than approving it.
Player 3: Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so both 0 and 3 look somewhat good though a single success is not conclusive. Player 4 now proposes [0,3,4], keeping the successful pair and adding themselves, which is a standard good proposal for Quest 2. As a Servant, the optimal play is usually to approve teams that contain previously successful members unless there is stronger contrary evidence. Approving reveals little beyond being consistent with pro-success play and helps the good side test 4 while preserving a trusted core.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are Evil, so 0 and 3 are very likely Good. The proposed team [0,3,4] contains no known Evil and extends the successful core [0,3] by adding me, which is optimal for Good on quest 2. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a strong clean team, and rejecting would risk unnecessary noise or worse teams later.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I’m Good with only quest outcomes to infer from. Teams [0,3] and [0,3,4] both succeeded with zero fails, so 0 and 3 look especially safe, and 4 is somewhat cleared as well. Current phase is team voting on my own proposed team [0,3] for quest 3; as Good, approving a compact team of previously successful players is optimal and avoids introducing less-cleared players. Approving reveals consistency with trusting proven-success teams, which helps Good more than it helps Evil here.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,3] contains no evil players and is very likely all-good given both 0 and 3 were on successful quests with no fails. As evil on quest 3 with Good already at 2 points, we must prevent another clean team from going through if possible. Voting reject is optimal because approving helps a probably-good team secure the game. My reject is somewhat consistent with evil trying to block trusted good cores, but one vote alone reveals limited information and the strategic need to stop this team outweighs concealment.
Player 2: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,3] contains no evil players and would guarantee a success, putting Good to 3-0 and close to victory. Players 0 and 3 have both been on successful teams, so they are likely trusted by Good; from an evil perspective, I should oppose pure-good teams whenever possible. In team voting, rejecting this team reveals limited information because evil can reasonably object to a 2-person team with only previously trusted members, and we need to prevent Good from chaining obvious success teams.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Both prior quests succeeded with teams [0,3] and [0,3,4], giving strongest good signals to 0,3(self),4, though double-evil could hide on quest 2. Proposed team [0,3] exactly repeats a proven-success pair from quest 1, which is very strong for a 2-person quest. As Good, optimal play is to approve highly trusted teams, especially at 2-0 where securing quest 3 wins before assassination pressure is ideal. My approve reveals I trust a previously successful core; that helps Good more than Evil here.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know players 1 and 2 are Evil, so players 0 and 3 are the safest good candidates besides me. Both prior successful quests used only players from {0,3,4} with zero fails, strongly indicating 0 and 3 are good. Current phase is team voting on quest 3; approving [0,3] is optimal because it excludes known evil and has already proven successful. My vote reveals little individually, and approving helps secure a likely winning third success for Good.
